comment.body:
My personal two cents: 
1. there will definitely be a correction
2. not exactly sure when it's gonna happen (but it will happen)
3. most likely there will be some external catalyst that trigger this (i.e. not a market initiated correction)

When the correction happens, that's when you buy and build your longer term investment positions.

So can't really time the market since correction will be triggered by external catalyst (maybe China).

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
mmm,
mean reversion here takes stationary, oscillating, theta decay day?
then it is high gamma exposures, and relatively steep short fly pattern..
or a very asymmetric speed profile, where the shorter gamma part is flat...
or a very narrow and steep long fly pattern.

if you ask non-stationary, the so-called "reversion trading"...
that is a strategy, when i don't go for the momentum, bcs it is uncertain, however if a certain line is broken, i see high odds for the spot to revert. 
you need low gamma, and steep local resistances.

for momentum day, we need to see a tight freeway towards any side. 
relatively flat speed profile... mostly long fly pattern, but not necessary.
like if i see long speed upwards, and relatively flat, it will let the spot to climb up, and will support every step up, like a ladder.
and if the downside is heavily sold, like the speed profile to the downside is steeper, i will bet on an upside momentum with higher confidence.

comment.body:
i lean more towards futures and stocks, rather than options. 
or vega neutral positions maybe if i think in yearly... but i don't hold yearly options ever.
quarterly expo at best (but very rarely), and only if i'm confident in the path.
options are not just about spot, but about volatility and time. you play for all these three .

i like to use options when a momentum ignites... or for hedging purposes on the longer trades.
or in weekly expos

comment.body:
it's the distance to the nearest static no-arbitrage wall, theoretically. up to the stock’s value after expected dividends for calls (bcs if there are no dividends, it can’t be worth more than the stock), and up to the present-value of the strike for puts. 
interestingly, SVI/SSVI vol surface fits are built to not let arb in calendar or butterflies either. https://arxiv.org/abs/1204.0646

comment.body:
i do it btw. 
longer-term strats it can be useful, and for predictions.
i say it so: for traders the 1st order greeks matter,
for market makers, the 2nd order greeks,
for analysts: the 3rd and 4th orders

bcs, we can state different path after deriving the expected dealer flow, how those flows gonna change.
